subject: Math

lesson.story_mission: Help the Ratio Rally team set fair prices, compare travel speeds, and label recipe mixes before the market opens.
lesson.objective: I can use ratio language, equivalent ratios, ratio tables, and unit rates to describe real situations.
lesson.concept_explanation: A ratio compares two quantities. Say the quantities in order, such as 3 to 4 or 3 for every 4. Equivalent ratios keep the same relationship. A unit rate compares a quantity to exactly 1 of another unit.
lesson.common_misconception: A common mistake is reversing the order of a ratio or mixing part-to-part and part-to-whole comparisons.
lesson.real_world_connection: Shoppers, runners, cooks, and readers use rates such as dollars per item, miles per hour, and pages per minute.
lesson.lesson_summary: Keep ratio order and units clear. Scale both numbers for equivalent ratios, and divide to find per 1.

page_audio.text: Read This Page. A ratio can compare part to part, like 3 red beads to 4 blue beads. A ratio can compare part to whole, like 3 red beads to 7 total beads. Equivalent ratios multiply or divide both numbers by the same amount, and a unit rate tells how much for 1.
page_audio.label: Read This Page

worked_examples.text: A club sells 12 dollars for 3 tickets. Divide 12 by 3 to find 4 dollars per ticket.

page_audio.text: Read This Page. Watch this unit rate example. Twelve dollars for 3 tickets means divide 12 dollars by 3 tickets. The answer is 4 dollars per ticket, which means 4 dollars for every 1 ticket.
page_audio.label: Read This Page

worked_examples.text: The ratio of lemons to limes is 3 to 4. Multiplying both numbers by 2 gives 6 to 8, an equivalent ratio.

page_audio.text: Read This Page. Watch the equivalent ratio table. Three lemons to 4 limes can become 6 lemons to 8 limes by multiplying both numbers by 2. The relationship stays the same.
page_audio.label: Read This Page

hint_ladder: Name both quantities in the same order as the question.
Use multiplication or division to keep the relationship the same.
Check the units: a unit rate always tells how much for 1.

guided_practice.prompt: There are 3 red flags and 4 blue flags. What is the ratio of red flags to blue flags?
hints: Name both quantities in the same order as the question.
Use multiplication or division to keep the relationship the same.
Check the units: a unit rate always tells how much for 1.

feedback.correct: Yes. Your ratio or rate keeps the quantities and units matched.
feedback.incorrect: Try again. Match the order, use equivalent ratios, and check the unit words.

feedback.correct: Yes. Your ratio or rate keeps the quantities and units matched.

misconception_bank.ratio_order_reversal: The ratio order was reversed. Say the quantities in the same order as the question.
misconception_bank.part_part_whole_confusion: A part-to-part ratio was mixed with a part-to-whole ratio. Identify whether the second number is another part or the total.
misconception_bank.equivalent_ratio_error: Both numbers were not multiplied or divided by the same factor. Scale both quantities together.
misconception_bank.unit_rate_confusion: The answer did not tell the amount for exactly 1 unit. Divide by the number of units and keep the unit words.

story.text: Read This Page. Welcome to the Ratio Rally market. Your team compares fruit baskets, tickets, miles, and reading speeds. Ratios tell how two quantities are related. Today you will keep the words and numbers in the same order.
story.label: Read This Page

lesson.text: Read This Page. A ratio can compare part to part, like 3 red beads to 4 blue beads. A ratio can compare part to whole, like 3 red beads to 7 total beads. Equivalent ratios multiply or divide both numbers by the same amount, and a unit rate tells how much for 1.
lesson.label: Read This Page

watch.text: Read This Page. Step one, read the quantities and units. Step two, write the ratio in the same order as the words. Step three, scale both numbers together or divide to find per 1. The final answer uses clear units, such as 4 dollars per ticket.
watch.label: Read This Page

demo.text: Read This Page. Watch the model connect the numbers. The table shows matching rows, the double number line lines up paired values, and the tape diagram keeps parts clear. Say the ratio naturally before you answer.
demo.label: Read This Page

practice.text: Read This Page. Practice one ratio question. Use the visual model first. Then tap Read Question to hear the question spoken naturally, and answer with the numbers and units in order.
practice.label: Read This Page

challenge.text: Read This Page. Challenge yourself with a rate question. Look for total amount and total units. Tap Read Question, divide to find per 1, and include the rate units.
challenge.label: Read This Page

checkpoint.text: Read This Page. This checkpoint checks your ratio and unit rate reasoning. Tap Read Question, explain which quantities match, and choose or type the answer that fits the model.
checkpoint.label: Read This Page

badge.text: Read This Page. You completed the Ratio Rally mission. Celebrate careful ratio language, equivalent ratios, ratio tables, and unit rates. Your badge shows you can reason with relationships.
badge.label: Read This Page

profile.text: Read This Page. Your profile saves stars, accuracy, hints, and misconception notes. Review any level that needs practice, then use Practice This Skill to strengthen ratios and unit rates.
profile.label: Read This Page
